Under configuration Capabilities - change request Management - Make Settings for Change transaction types and Copy control Rules should be add date type from and to value SRV_RREADY.

entry.title: How SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Tax and Revenue Management Could Enhance Tax Administration
entry.content:
Unlocking the Power of Data for Tax Administration

Tax agencies worldwide face increasing pressure to enhance compliance, detect fraud earlier, and optimise collections, all while managing vast amounts of structured and unstructured data. However, simply collecting more data is not enough, agencies need the ability to analyse it effectively, connect relevant insights, and act on real-time risks. With SAP Tax and Revenue Management (SAP TRM) on S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, tax agencies already have a trusted system of record for managing taxpayer obligations, compliance, and collections. Embedded machine learning capabilities such as SAP Behavioural Insights provide predictive analytics that help agencies:

  • Forecast late payments, allowing proactive taxpayer engagement.
  • Detect non-compliance patterns, helping compliance officers focus on high-risk cases.
  • Personalise engagement strategies, ensuring tailored interventions for different taxpayer segments.

However, while SAP TRM provides critical insights into taxpayer behaviour, it operates primarily within SAP system data. Compliance decisions often require a broader, real-time view, integrating external datasets such as banking transactions, business registries, and customs records.

This is where SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and Joule AI agents could complement SAP TRM providing seamless data integration and AI-driven insights to help tax authorities make better, data-driven decisions.

 

How SAP Business Data Cloud Could Support Tax Agencies

Many tax agencies struggle with data silos, making it difficult to gain a complete view of taxpayer compliance. SAP Business Data Cloud provides a scalable, cloud-based infrastructure for data products that could unify SAP TRM with financial transactions, e-commerce platforms, and customs data. As an example, with BDC, tax authorities could gain deeper insights into taxpayer cash flow, identify potentially unregistered businesses operating in the digital economy, and automate cross-checking of VAT refund claims against customs records.

By integrating structured and unstructured tax data and providing it as a harmonised data product, BDC ensures tax authorities have the most complete, context-rich datasets available

How Will Joule AI Agents and SAP Business Data Cloud Work Together?

SAP Business Data Cloud provides the trusted data foundation, while Joule AI agents act as the intelligence layer, analysing information and surfacing insights to support tax officers. By leveraging real-time data from SAP TRM and external sources, Joule AI agents could help tax agencies detect anomalies, assess risks, and prioritise compliance actions, ensuring enforcement remains data-driven while under human oversight.

With access to a more complete dataset through BDC, Joule AI agents could process large volumes of tax data and provide insights that help tax officers focus on the most relevant cases. This includes analysing patterns in taxpayer behaviour, identifying potential risks before they escalate, and offering recommendations that improve compliance strategies. 

Many tax agencies still rely on manual enforcement, requiring tax officers to review cases, issue notices, and escalate compliance actions. AI agents could help agencies prioritise high-risk cases while ensuring fairness and consistency.

For example, tax agencies could (where legally permitted):

  • Fraud Detection in VAT Refunds – AI-driven models could analyse VAT refund claims alongside customs and supplier transaction data to detect inconsistencies before refunds are processed.
  • Identify potentially unregistered businesses by analysing payment platform activity.
  • Detect inconsistencies in tax declarations by linking structured revenue data with third-party business activity.
  • Shadow Economy Risk Indicators – BDC could integrate social, economic, and transaction data to help tax agencies identify unregistered businesses.
  • Simulate the impact of enhanced compliance on the tax base and tax rates, supporting tax reform efforts

By acting as the foundation for AI-driven insights, BDC ensures that tax agencies access complete, trusted data to support compliance and fraud detection initiatives.

 

The Role of SAP Knowledge Graph in AI Decision-Making

So where does SAP Knowledge Graph fit into this picture? For AI-generated insights to be reliable and actionable, they must understand how different datasets relate to each other. SAP Knowledge Graph, embedded within SAP Business Data Cloud, can enables this by mapping relationships between tax records, transactions, and regulatory data.

With SAP Knowledge Graph, Joule AI agents could deliver process-aware insights ensuring compliance recommendations are business context aware. For example:

  • Understand connections between taxpayer records, transactions, and regulatory data.
  • Enhance AI-driven decision-making, improving fraud detection, risk scoring, and tax collection efforts.

BDC combined with SAP Knowledge Graph ensures that Joule AI agents generate AI insights that are grounded in business reality rather than isolated data points.

The Future of AI-Driven Tax Administration

As tax agencies continue modernising their compliance and fraud detection strategies, the combination of SAP Business Data Cloud, Joule AI agents, and SAP Tax and Revenue Management could provide a strong foundation for AI-assisted decision-making.

With access to trusted, high-quality enterprise data, tax officers could prioritise compliance cases more effectively, ensuring resources are focused where they are needed most. AI-driven insights could help improve collections by identifying taxpayers at risk of late payments and recommending appropriate actions, such as structured installment plans. In dispute resolution, AI could accelerate case handling by surfacing the most relevant information from tax records, taxpayer interactions, and supporting documents.

Finally, governance and compliance remain central to this transformation. AI-driven insights must align with GDPR, tax laws, and regulatory policies, ensuring transparency and fairness. Tax agencies must retain oversight, with AI assisting tax officers rather than replacing human judgment, ensuring decisions remain proportionate and legally sound.

This is just the beginning. More to come.

Please contact Minesh Ladwa for more information

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: Calculating a Corporate Carbon Footprint
entry.content:
Disclaimer: This blog post reflects the state of the SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) as of March 10th, 2025.

In today's business world, sustainability has become a critical focus, not just due to regulatory demands but also from genuine corporate accountability and environmental stewardship. With both intrinsic motivations to reduce carbon emissions and regulatory requirements such as the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS E1-6) mandating the disclosure of Scope 1, 2, and 3 total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, companies face an urgent call to action. SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) offers a comprehensive solution to support businesses in this dual pursuit, providing a robust tool for calculating and managing their corporate carbon footprint (CCF). By leveraging SCT, organizations can align their sustainability strategies with both their own environmental goals and compliance needs. This article explores a simple example on how SCT empowers businesses to seamlessly integrate sustainability into their core operations, meeting regulatory standards while advancing their intrinsic commitment to a sustainable future.

Before you start with the CCF calculation you need to have completed the technical setup and have the appropriate user roles. For details, see:

  • Enabling Corporate Carbon Footprint Calculation
  • User Roles

This CCF process can be divided into 4 subtasks:

  1. Set-up relevant Master Data

Manage Master Data: SCT allows you to set-up your company hierarchy by means of the Legal Entity (ID_COMPANY_CODE), Reporting Line (ID_ORG_UNIT) and Business Location (ID_BUSINESS_LOC). It is important to note that CCF is based on Business Locations. Therefore, you need to ensure that Business Locations are maintained. In this example we will focus on two Business Locations, Munich and Berlin:

Besides Business Locations it is important to note that SAP relies on the Emissions DPI to store the GHG information. All relevant dimensions such as Allocation Method, Scope, Scope Category, Scope Subcategory are already maintained by SAP.

Manage Emission Factors: In the Manage Emissions Factors app you can see that SCT comes pre-delivered with Emission Factor (EF) data packages by the US government Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Beyond that it is possible to provide own EF data package. Therefore, an LCA Template or a Simple Template can be filled out. For calculating the CCF, the Simple Template is recommended.

Mandatory fields in the template are highlighted with *. In this simplified example you can find for instance the data package entry 1003 “Train Travel” with the UNIT_OF_MEASURE in KM, where 100KM in DE (Germany) result in 0.56 kgCO2e.

 

Once you have set-up the data package, you can release it.

  1. Periodic Configuration

Manage Business Activities: A Business Activity can be any activity in your company that you want to calculate emissions for. One activity is Business Location specific. If you want to calculate your Scope 3.6 Business Travel emissions across your company, you will need to create the respective Business Activities for each Business Location.

In our simplified example we want to calculate the emissions from the following Business Activities from Berlin and Munich. You can therefore create the Business Activities directly in SCT or download a template and upload it, which will make sense for bigger organizations.

Let’s add another Business Activity for Train Travel for Munich. For the name of the Business Activity, it makes sense to include the location ID. You can select your GHG Category, Description, Location, Validity Period and the Emission Factor Periods.

Once you have updated the General Information part, you can maintain the Emission Factor Period.  

As we have provided the Emission Factor for Train Travel in our CUSTOMER_SPECIFIC data package, we can select it and relevant fields such as the Quantity, Quantity UoM and Total CO2e are prefilled automatically from the data package.

You can then select the Preferred Footprint Type from Quantity-Based, Spend-Based or Manual Emission Footprint. In this case we only maintain the Quantity-Based Footprint. If you have provided also the Spend-Based Footprint in your Emission Factor data package, you can maintain both Footprint Details the Business Activity.

Manage GHG Data Collections: Before you can finally start with the data collection and calculation itself you need to set-up the GHG Data Collection(s). With that you define the period and the Business Activities you want to collect data for every Business Location. In this case, The Footprint Inventory Scope has been created for Periods January, February and March whereas the first two months of 2024 have already been completed with the data collection.

When clicking into the newly created Footprint Inventory Scope, SCT sets up the GHG Data Forms for every Business Location. In our case Los Angeles, Mexico City and New York are not relevant, so we can easily scope them out by setting them to not applicable.

Furthermore, you can notice that after clicking on the Footprint Inventory Scope you have actually changed the App and are now in the GHG Data Collection. You can now click on any Business Location where you want to start your data collection for.

 

  1. Collection and Calculation

Record GHG Data: If you want to collect your GHG Data, you have two options to get there. 1) as described above via the Manage GHG Data Collections App and then clicking on the respective Footprint Inventory Scope, in this case March 2024 or 2) via Record GHG Data Data App:

When clicking into one Business Location, you can then edit the entry and collect the data.

After entering the data, SCT might give a notification. In this case it notifies the user that the entered quantity differs by more than 30% compared to the previous period. In this case March 2024 is compared to February 2024 for the Business Location Berlin.

When you have provided both options to maintain the data Quantity-Based and Spend-Based, you can decide how you want to collect your data. In this case for Scope 3.6 long haul travel you can decide whether you want to enter the data via the flown kilometers, or the Euros spent. Once you have maintained one option, you cannot maintain the other one anymore.

  At the end you can save your data collection. After collecting all the relevant data and saving it you can see that the progress is now at 100% and you can set the data collection to complete.

Finally, in the Manage GHG Data Collections App you can review the data a final time and confirm the review. Once confirmed, the data collection is completed and reviewed and can then be visualized in SCT.

  1. Reporting and Analytics

You can see that the data collection has been successfully completed in the following Apps: Manage ESG Data, Analyze ESG Data, Our Ambitions and in View Emission Dashboard:

entry.title: SAP Business Rules - BTP service is deprecated
entry.content:
Hello,
We have been dealing with the fact that the constant changes in services on SAP BTP are a significant problem for our customers and our projects.

Now it has happened that the SAP Business Rules service has been marked as Deprecated without any information about what its successor is, or how we can adequately address a replacement in another way.

@SAPBTPDev @sapbtp Could you please advise us on where and how we can find information regarding this unexpected activity (decision)? I would expect some OSS note, but nothing is mentioned anywhere. The service simply disappeared from the Discovery Center - I would expect all information there, including the General OSS Note and instructions for the next steps.

Thank you,
Jiri

entry.title: Accessibility in AI: Designing Solutions for Everyone
entry.content:
Side profile of a person with dark hair and beard, eyes closed, wearing a white t-shirt, holding a phone with one hand while the index finger of the other hand is positioned just above the screen.

At SAP, we believe accessibility is more than a compliance standard: Accessibility is a fundamental human right. SAP’s Global Human Rights Commitment Statement underscores our efforts to build a digital world that is more inclusive, accessible, and equitable for all. As artificial intelligence (AI) continues transforming workplaces and society, our updated Global AI Ethics Policy reaffirms that accessibility is a matter of fairness and respect for human dignity.

Making AI Work for Everyone

AI is transforming how the world interacts with digital technologies—from workplace tools to everyday applications.  When accessibility is not prioritized during design and development, millions worldwide cannot access these powerful solutions. This disparity makes accessibility essential to ethical AI development, which in turn compels people-centered companies like SAP to create solutions that empower every individual, regardless of their abilities or circumstances.

AI’s emerging challenges push industries worldwide to rethink how we approach accessibility in AI development. As AI reshapes the digital landscape, we must think beyond traditional accessibility requirements to create AI-driven solutions that intentionally include and work well for our diverse global community.

Why Accessibility Matters in SAP’s Business AI

AI is part of many business applications that individuals use daily, from customer service and sales to workplace productivity. Making these applications accessible means everyone can use them for work and daily tasks. With thoughtful design and emerging technologies, AI applications have significant potential to enhance accessibility and streamline interactions for all users. However, when AI applications aren’t accessible, they can create barriers for people, limiting their ability to engage with digital services, contribute to the workforce, make informed decisions, or access critical information.

Consider someone using an AI-powered medical app to share confidential updates with their physician about an ongoing condition but finding their screen reader can’t access all screens or detect critical AI responses, forcing them to share private health information with technical support teams. Or imagine a new hire who can’t complete the onboarding process through the company’s AI-enhanced platform because the steps aren’t keyboard accessible. This could require them to disclose their disability to human resources before they’re ready and may negatively affect their initial workplace experience. Without thoughtful accessibility consideration, these powerful technologies meant to simplify life might instead create new barriers, affecting everything from privacy and career advancement to workplace inclusion and full participation in society. As conversational interfaces and AI technologies continue to evolve, they have the potential to make digital experiences more intuitive and accessible for everyone. These advances can open new opportunities for workplace participation and help people use services independently.

SAP views accessibility in Business AI as fundamentally about people—helping everyone effectively use our AI solutions, regardless of ability. Making AI applications accessible requires deliberate planning and the right tools. By building accessibility into our Business AI solutions from the start, we strive to create AI solutions that enable more people to benefit from these technological advances. This approach helps create a future where AI enhances human capability and upholds dignity for all users.

SAP’s Approach to Building Accessibility into Business AI

SAP weaves a commitment to accessibility into the fabric of our AI Ethics Policy, especially our principle of Fairness and Non-Discrimination. While we continuously enhance all our AI systems to meet rigorous accessibility standards, this commitment goes beyond corporate compliance; it’s about enabling everyone, particularly people with disabilities, to fully participate in and benefit from our AI solutions. 

We offer our developers and designers several resources to bring this commitment to life in our products, including:

  • Inclusive Research Handbook. Understanding how people use our products is essential for accessibility. Our Inclusive Research Handbook guides teams across our product portfolio to recognize diverse user needs and perspectives. People approach technology in different ways, often influenced by their individual circumstances and needs. Referring to these insights during each step of product development helps us create AI solutions that meet the diverse needs of users worldwide.
  • Accessibility Design Tools. To support SAP teams as they build accessibility into their development and design processes, we provide the Accessibility Design Tools Handbook. This guide offers practical steps for meeting accessibility standards and helps teams work more efficiently by considering accessibility throughout product development.
  • SAP UI5. SAP UI5 has built-in accessibility features that have been part of our design system for many years. SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad incorporate SAP UI5, providing developers and designers with tools and guidance to create experiences that work for everyone. These resources help us create AI business products and services that respect and empower all users.

People First, Always

The importance of considering diverse needs in AI development extends far beyond business applications. As AI becomes more prevalent in everyday applications, accessibility isn’t just about compliance; it’s about digital inclusion and human dignity. Through intentional design and development that considers diverse needs, SAP strives to create AI solutions that serve and empower users globally now and in the future.

Want to Learn More About How SAP Creates Business Software That Works for Everyone?

  • SAP Accessibility: Learn how we’re making our products more accessible, especially for people with disabilities.
  • SAP Responsible AI: Discover how our AI Ethics principles put the well-being of people at the center of everything we do.

entry.title: SAP IBP Configuration - Create a Version in SAP IBP
entry.content:
In SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP), a version refers to a specific instance or snapshot of planning data at a given point in time. Versions are used to manage different scenarios and simulations within the planning process. Here are some key points about versions in SAP IBP:

  • Scenario Planning: Versions allow you to create and compare different planning scenarios. For example, you can have a baseline version and several alternative versions to evaluate the impact of different strategies.
  • What-If Analysis: By using versions, you can perform what-if analyses to see how changes in demand, supply, or other factors affect your overall plan.
  • Collaboration: Different teams can work on their own versions and then merge or compare them to make informed decisions.
  • Version Management: SAP IBP provides tools to manage versions, including creating, copying, and deleting versions as needed.

Versions vs Scenarios vs Snapshots

In SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP), versions and scenarios are both used for planning and analysis, but they serve different purposes and have distinct characteristics:

Versions

  • Purpose: Versions are used for structured scenario planning and what-if analysis across the organization. They represent different sets of planning data that can be compared and analyzed.
  • Usage: Versions allow you to create multiple planning scenarios, such as best-case, worst-case, and baseline scenarios. Each version can have different key figure values and can be used to evaluate the impact of various strategies.
  • Visibility: Versions are typically visible to all users with the necessary permissions and can be managed at the company or department level.
  • Management: Versions are created and managed by administrators in the configuration settings.

Scenarios

  • Purpose: Scenarios are used for user-specific, ad-hoc what-if analysis. They allow individual planners to simulate changes without affecting the baseline data.
  • Usage: Scenarios enable planners to make temporary changes to the planning data to see how these changes affect the overall plan. These changes are not saved to the baseline but can be saved as a scenario for future reference.
  • Visibility: Scenarios are user-specific and are not visible to other users unless shared. This allows planners to work independently without impacting others' work.
  • Management: Scenarios can be created on the fly by any user with the appropriate permissions, directly within the planning view.

In summary, versions are used for broader, structured planning and are visible to multiple users, while scenarios are used for individual, temporary simulations and are user-specific.

Snapshots

  • Purpose: Snapshots capture key figure values at a specific point in time. They are used to track and compare historical data over time.
  • Usage: Snapshots are useful for monitoring how key figures evolve throughout the planning cycle. They can be taken periodically to capture the state of the data at different times.
  • Visibility: Snapshots are stored as snapshot key figures and can be displayed alongside other key figures in planning views.

In summary, versions are used for creating and comparing different planning scenarios, while snapshots are used for capturing and comparing historical data at specific points in time.

Create a version in the Web UI using the version ID UPSIDE.

1. In planning area's configuration, choose Versions tab and choose New to create a version.

2. Enter ID, Name, and Description.

3. Add Key Figures to Version.

4. Select Key Figures to add to version

5. Save version.

entry.title: SPS updating of the ASE database through the sap host control command.
entry.content:
Greetings, BASIS/ASE DBA community.

On this article, I'd like to explore a step-by-step procedure for updating ASE DB on a Windows environment.

Current DB Version:  16.0 SP03 PL14

Updated DB Version: 16.0 SP04 PL06 HF1

Operating System: Windows Server 2019

We should patch our SAP-ASE (Adaptive Server Enterprise) Sybase database from time to time as recommended by SAP.

This helps in correcting bugs in certain patch levels

Below pre-requisites should be maintained for updating ASE DB with SAPHOSTAGENT

  • SAP-ASE version must be 15.7 SP100 or higher
  • SAP Host Agent version must be 7.21 PL43 or higher
  • Make sure that no active clients are connected to SAP-ASE and no automated actions are scheduled while running the update.

A few definitions relating to minor, major upgrades, BuS, and nonBuS.

Minor Update: Updates within SAP-ASE 15.7 or 16.0 (eg. 16.0.02.04 to 16.0.03.06) are named minor updates.

Major Update: Updates from SAP-ASE 15.7 to 16.0 and higher are named major updates (eg. 15.7.0.138 to 16.0.02.04)

BuS: Using SAP ASE together with SAP Business Suite shall be named BuS.

nonBuS: While running any other application on SAP-ASE shall be named nonBuS.

The update progress is written to the LiveUpdate.log file in the SAP Host Agent work directory and can be looked up there.

In Windows: Drive<Name>: Program files\SAP\hostctrl\work

In Linux: /usr/sap/hostctrl/work

The update generates logs in:

C:\Program Files\SAP\hostctrl\work\liveupdate.log
<drive>:\sybase\<SID>\sapdbctrl-config\ASE_UPDATE.dat
<drive>:\sybase\<SID>\log\ASE.log
C:\Program Files\SAP\hostctrl\work\dev_sapdbctrl

The SAP Host Agent saves the update progress and in case the update is interrupted, restarting the update will continue from the last task executed. Once the update process has replaced the software, you should no longer cancel the update but rather finish the update, if necessary, by skipping failing tasks.

Step-1: To get the database version, execute the following command and Check the OS/DB compatibility in PAM

F:\sybase\GMK>isql -S<SID> -U<db_user_name> -P<Password> -X -w9999

1> select @@version

2>go                                                                                                                                                                       ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Adaptive Server Enterprise/16.0 SP03 PL14/EBF 30731 SMP/P/AMD64/Windows 2008 R2 SP1/ase160sp03pl14x/0/64-bit/FBO/Wed May 10 14:16:43 2

Step-2: Download the required DB patches from SAP portal and place them at "<Drive>:\DB_UPG"

SAP Support Portal Home–> Software downloads–> Databases –> Database and Database Patches –>SAP ADAPTIVE SERVER ENTERPRISE –> DATABASE PATCHES –> SAP ASE FOR BUSINESS SUITE –> SAP-ASE 16.0 FOR BUS. SUITE –> Select the required patch by filter with DB OS Type

Step-3: SAP Host Agent version must be 7.21 PL43 or higher

If not, please update the hostagent to latest version.

Follow the below article to automate the upgrade of SAP host agent 

Auto-Upgrade of SAPHOST-AGENT: - SAP Community

Step-4:  In worst case, if you need to roll back to the old existing version of SAP-ASE, then you must take a backup of %SYBASE% directory.

Unix: /sybase/<SID>
Windows: <drive>:/Sybase/<SID>

For Windows, you may use “Windows Server Backup” application to take a local backup of Sybase directory.

Step-5: Validate the content of SAP Secure stores with below command.

C:\Program Files\SAP\hostctrl\exe/saphostctrl -user sapadm <sapadm_user_password> -function LiveDatabaseUpdate -dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb -updateoption TASK=VALIDATE_SEC_STORES

The returned values are: [GLOBAL | LOCAL]_<AUTHENTICATION>=[not found | invalid | valid | empty | set]

Not found:  Secure Store does not exist or cannot be read. Or authentication entry is not maintained.  

Invalid: Maintained credentials could not be confirmed.

Valid: Maintained credentials could be confirmed.

Empty: Authentication was read but credentials are empty.

Set: Unix only! Credentials are maintained, but as OS user switch on Unix can be done without password. No password is checked.

If any account is missing, then you can add them with the following command to the list of the sap host agent.

Executing the following command will return a list of users and their credentials which are available in the secure store.

For SA user:

  • rsecssfx get DB_CONNECT/SYB/SADB_USER
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SADB_USER sapsa -plain
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SADB_PASSWORD <sapsa_user_password>
  • saphostctrl -user sapadm <sapadm_user_password> -function SetDatabaseProperty -dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb -dbuser sapsa -dbpass <sa_user_password> USER=SAPSA

For SAPSSO user:

  • rsecssfx get DB_CONNECT/SYB/SSODB_USER
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SSODB_USER sapsso -plain
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SSODB_PASSWORD <sapsso_user_password>
  • saphostctrl -user sapadm <sapadm_user_password> -function SetDatabaseProperty -dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb -dbuser sapsso -dbpass <sapsso_user_password> USER=SAPSSO

For SA user:

  • rsecssfx get DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAUPDDB_USER
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAUPDDB_USER sa -plain
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAUPDDB_PASSWORD <sa_user_password>
  • saphostctrl -user sapadm <sapadm_user_password> -function SetDatabaseProperty -dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb -dbuser sa -dbpass <sa_user_password> USER=SAUPD

For SYBSID user:

  • rsecssfx get DB_CONNECT/SYB/SYBSID_USER
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SYBSID_USER <Domain>\sybsid -plain
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SYBSID_PASSWORD <sybsid_user_password>
  • saphostctrl -user sapadm <sapadm_user_password> -function SetDatabaseProperty -dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb -dbuser <Domain\sybsid> -dbpass <sybsid_user_password> USER=SYBSID

For SAPSID user:

  • rsecssfx get DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAPSID_USER
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAPSID_USER <Domain\sapsid> -plain
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAPSID_PASSWORD <sapsid_user_password>
  • saphostctrl -user sapadm <sapadm_user_password> -function SetDatabaseProperty -dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb -dbuser <Domain\sapsid> -dbpass <sapsid_user_password> USER=SAPSID

For sapadm user:

  • rsecssfx get DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAPADM_USER
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAPADM_USER sapadm -plain
  • rsecssfx put DB_CONNECT/SYB/SAPADM_PASSWORD <sapadm_user_password>
  • saphostctrl -user sapadm <sapadm_user_password> -function SetDatabaseProperty -dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb -dbuser sapadm -dbpass <sapadm_user_password> USER=SAPADM

Adding sapsso user credentials and validation

Verifying sapsso user validation in secure store
Check the validation for sapsso user in the secure store

Adding sapadm user entry and password validation in secure store

Verifying sapdm user validation in secure store

Step-6:  Stop all applications running on the SAP-ASE server and make sure no jobs are scheduled for the time of the update. To enforce that no jobs are running you can stop job scheduler before starting the update (SAP Host Agent 7.21 SP44 and higher will do this either way After the system is checked and no active database connections were found).

Step-7:  Execute below command to check if the upgrade will be successful or will it fail due to some error. It’s just a check and it won’t actually update any files.

saphostctrl -function LiveDatabaseUpdate –dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb  -updatemethod Check -updateoption TASK=CHECK_UPDATE_ASE -updateoption DROP_LOCATION=”<Drive>:\DB_UPG”

If any client connections are still present, you will receive a warning similar to this

Step-8:  Execute the below command to update ASE DB

saphostctrl -function LiveDatabaseUpdate –dbname <SAPSID> -dbtype syb -updatemethod Execute -updateoption TASK=UPDATE_ASE -updateoption DROP_LOCATION=”<Drive>:\DB_UPG” 

  1. INIT_UPDATE_ASE
     Internal preparations for the SAP ASE update.
  2. CHECK_UPDATE_ASE
    Check user input (eg. software package specified in DROP_LOCATION).
    Check compatibility of package (with asemanifest.mf).
    Check that /tmp is not mounted with no exec property (prevents setup.bin from being executed properly).
    Check that SAP-ASE is up and running in case update is initial.
    Check that no clients are connected to SAP-ASE.
    Check if major update is restarted with wrong software package.
    Check if required credentials are valid (sapsid, sapsa, sapsso, sa, sybsid, SAPSR3, DR_admin).
    Check-in case of replication that site is standby or option UPDATE_PRIMARY=1 is setup.
    Check if the SAP-ASE runs with BuS or nonBuS application.
    Check that PATCH.SAR exists in case SAP-ASE runs with BuS application.
    If hadr_mode != 1 set 'grant allow hadr login to sso_role' (SAP-ASE 15.7.0.136 does not have it but requires it).
    Check CDS object requirements are met.
    Check without error that sybsystemprocs & sybmgmtdb have the recommended size (you might want to increase the size before/after the update).
  3. UPDATE_ASE_PRE_CONFIG
    If required configure database options to support the update to target version.
    Disable job scheduler.
    Drop     <SID>_XP and SYB_EJB servers for major update on BuS.
    If major update check and set     net password encryption required.
    Disable auditing.
    If major update unlock     sa.
    If BuS enforce dboption     ddl in tran to be true in SID database.
  4. UPDATE_ASE_SOFTWARE
    Stop SAP ASE servers.
    If major update copy     sysam directory.
    If on AIX run     slibclean.
    Update software with Sybase installer.
    If BuS and target SAP-ASE version is SP03 or higher install     PATCH.SAR contents.
    If BuS and target SAP-ASE version is SP03 or higher and OS is Unix, change     sapsid user env for ODBC (dbenv.sh/csh).
  5. UPDATE_ASE_POST_CONFIG
    If major update and OS is Windows Update user profile environments in registry
    Start the SAP-ASE servers.
    If major update run     sybatch/sqlupgraderes tool for SAP-ASE server.
    If major update run     sybatch/sqlupgraderes tool for backup server.
    If BuS and OS is Windows delete XP service.
    If major update renames obsolete     15_0 directories to 15_0_OLD and restart SAP-ASE servers (make sure changes in env & on disk grab the right libraries).
    If required lock     sa.
    Check and reset     net password encryption required.
    Execute SAP ASE install scripts which are not default to     sybatch/sqlupgraderes but recommended (eg. installdbextend).
    Enable job scheduler.
  6. UPDATE_ASE_BS_DEFAULTS
    If required update remaining database options to support the update to target version.
    Execute     syb_update_db.txt script to set BuS defaults.
  7. RECREATE_CDS_OBJECTS
    If target is SP03 recreate CUDFs.
    If target is SP02PL04 or SP03 and source is lower than target or force recreate GTTs.
    If target is SP02PL04 or SP03 and source is lower than target or force recreate TUDFs.
    If target is SP03PL06 and source is SP03PL03 or target > SP03PL03 and (source is SP03PL03 or < SP03PL03) or force inline TUDFs.
  8. SETUP_BALDR
    If target SAP-ASE version is SP03 or higher execute instbaldr script else setup DCF
    (wrapper for     SetDatabaseProperty SETUP_LIST_METRICS=saptools).
  9. CONFIG_GP
    Configure and enable granular permissions (replication will be turned off for this step).
  10. obsolete: REPLACE_DBI
    Close all database connections between SAP Host Agent and SAP-ASE/RMA.
    Unload the database drivers.
    Remove/rename the local libraries.
    Copy the required files from OCS folders to SAP Host Agent exe folder.
  11. RESET_UPDATE_ASE
    Backup files in sapdbctrl-config.
    Finish the SAP-ASE update.

Final restart of the database for further usage

Check the DB version

Documentation below can be referred for more information.

  1. Targeted ASE 16.x Release Schedule and CR list Information | SAP Help Portal
  2. 2800483 - SYB: Upgrade / Update SAP ASE with SAP Host Agent 7.21 PL43 and later - SAP for Me
  3. 1797040 - SYB: SAP Host Agent - Using global or local secure storage - SAP for Me

 

Thanks & Regards,
Mani

entry.title: Static Rounding Profile
entry.content:
While managing customer orders in the system, in some process-based scenarios, it may be necessary to round the order quantity received from the customer due to requirements such as ensuring compatibility with package quantity arrangements. By assigning a specific coefficient, the entered order quantity can be rounded according to the relevant coefficient ratio. This rounding process can be carried out within the standard SAP structure by following the steps below.

Definition of Rounding Profile

Transaction code: OWD1

Configuration step: SPRO -> Material Management -> Purchasing -> Purchase Order Optimization -> Quantity Optimization and Available Logistic Units of Measure -> Maintenance of Rounding Profile


In the system, two different rounding profile types can be defined as shown in the image.

Static Rounding Profile

In Static Rounding Profile definition, a threshold value is set to perform basic rounding. The threshold value is defined at the plant level. No specific unit of measure is considered while defining the threshold value. When the entered order quantity exceeds the specified threshold, the system rounds it up to the next value. Once a rounding profile is defined for a plant, it can be used for different plants as well.

Dynamic Rounding Profile

In defining a dynamic rounding profile, besides considering the unit of measurement, it also offers different features. In defining dynamic profile, rounding method is selected and rounding rule is defined. In the rounding rule, percentage calculation is made on the basis of unit of measure. Similarly, the dynamic rounding profile is defined at the production site level and can be used at more than one production site.

"Static rounding profile" definition steps below:

"Threshold value" and "Rounding value" is selected.

Threshold value -> Order quantity that can be entered without rounding

Rounding value -> It is the value that should be rounded after the threshold value is passed. If the amount is entered over the rounding value, the system will perform the update process as multiples of the rounding value.

In the related example above, all orders entered over 1 quantity are rounded to 100.

Note: As mentioned above, the system does not take into account any unit of measure in the static rounding profile.

Item type rounding value configuration

Transaction code: VOV7

1) In order for rounding to be active on item type basis, the following item type maintenance is performed.

2) The rounding profile created in the system must be assigned in the material master.

In the above process, the rounding profile defined for the material.

3) As soon as the sales order is entered, the system revises the quantity by considering the rounding profile.

Simulation Process

There is also a "Simulation" button on the profile definition screen. The system simulates the quantity based on the rounding profile maintenance determined without even opening an order.

The system simulates that the value to be rounded for the entered amount is 100.

When the test is performed with a value above 100, the result is as follows;

The system simulates that the value to be rounded for the entered amount is 200.

entry.title: IBP S&OP Operator - PCAPAUSAGE not being calculated
entry.content:
We run an monthly heuristic (infinite supply). Last month when it was run, PCAPAUSAGE was populated with capacity requirements. As last month's run was prior to the 2502 release, I am wondering if something changed which is causing this issue. I reviewed the release notes but do not see anything that would cause this issue.

Does anyone have any idea why this would be happening?

UPDATE: I found that PCAPACONSUMPTION is null and that may be causing the issue,

entry.title: Re: How to know how the upgrade was done ?
entry.content: Until the last SP is transported into production, you're in the middle of an SAP upgrade. It's customary to keep development to a minimum during these phases, and it's even worse if you only have one development system.

entry.title: Re: Intercompany matching and reconciliation (ICMR) - per...
entry.content:
Dear GraceZou,
Thanks for the information provided so far! I am sure that it is useful for the SAP community around the world!

One question from my side - since some clients do not want to use the standard reconciliation close solution (mainly because of the restrictions mentioned in OSS note 3096908:
"Customers can close the reconciliation period to prevent further data inflow for documents matching. However, the prevention is only valid when the closed period is the current period, based on your system time and the fiscal year variant. Which also means if now is the beginning of a new period, you can still run matching and roll in data even the prior period is closed."), I would like to ask if there is a BADI, enhancement point where a custom validation with a Z* table can be implemented, checking if you are allowed to run "auto-matching" for the selected Fiscal Year Period? Any enhancement hints related to "auto-matching" in the ICMR Manage Assignments apps will be highly appreciated.

Thanks and Regards,

Yanislav

entry.title: Defining Selection Variants for Shipments
entry.content:
Hi Community of SAP

You will explore how to define selection criteria for selecting deliveries to be assigned to a shipment.

You can select deliveries, for example, according to shipping point, due date, and status of the delivery. “Delivery” means return deliveries or incoming deliveries. this selection variant sets the criteria for deliveries to be added to the transport and makes it work faster

 

SAP IMG (SPRO)

Logistics Execution-Transportation-Shipments-Define Shipment Types

 

First, changes can be made to an existing shipment type or a new shipment type can be defined here

Enter the relevant shipment type with double click

After making the relevant configurations in the shipment type, go to the proposal section at the bottom

enter a variant name for the new variant to be created and click maintain

After clicking maintain, the following screen will open. here you can create a variant for the deliveries to be connected to the shipment. it can be created by giving data such as shipping point, loading point, loading date …

In addition, if “include deliveries in proceed shipment” is checked, the delivery to be connected to the shipment will be automatically assigned according to the selected criteria.

But if a condition is written behind the shipping exits, it will be assigned directly to the shipment without detecting it. for example; if the delivery is in credit blockage and a code is written that a delivery in credit blockage should not be assigned to shipping, it will not enter this code and will pass directly to the assigned side.

this credit blockage may cause problems by being noticed in the following processes. or if the delivery that is automatically assigned is pulled back to the not assgined lower part and assigned to the upper part again, the error written in the exit can only be noticed in that way and this will cause a big waste of time.

if “include deliveries in proceed shipment” is not checked, the delivery will appear below as unassigned. and can be assigned with user control

In addition, Ovrl Goods Mvt Sts and or transportation planning status can be included in the variant by giving an additional filter here.

if we want to make a field mandatory in the variant selection, click on the attributes button

description is entered and we can check which fields should be mandatory in the delivery selection screens.

When the shipment creation screen is checked again, it can be seen that the field selected in the selection variant is mandatory.

additionally created variants can be seen in the selection variant section of the VT01N input screen

You can examine the program of the created variant by entering the SAPLV56L related program at se38.

I would be happy to read your thoughts and comments, and if there are different comments and ways, write them in the comments.

 

Best Regards

entry.title: Enhancing IDoc Filtering in SAP BD64
entry.content:
A Step-by-Step Guide

Introduction

In SAP, IDocs (Intermediate Documents) serve as a crucial mechanism for data exchange between systems. However, sending all IDocs without filtering can lead to unnecessary data transmission and performance issues. SAP provides filtering options in BD64 to ensure that only relevant IDocs are sent based on specific criteria.

Understanding IDoc Filtering in BD64

Filtering in BD64 (Distribution Model) allows SAP users to define conditions that control IDoc transmission. For example:

  • MATMAS IDocs (Material Master) can be filtered by Material Type (MTART), Plant (WERKS), Storage Location (LGORT), etc.
  • DEBMAS IDocs (Customer Master) can be filtered by Sales Organization, Distribution Channel, etc.

If an IDoc does not meet the filter conditions, it is not sent to the target system.

Step-by-Step Configuration of IDoc Filtering in BD64

  1. Define the Filtering Fields in BD59.
  • Go to Transaction BD59.
  • Select the Standard Message Type (e.g., MATMAS for Material Master).
  • Identify the relevant segments and fields for filtering.
  • Example: Material Type (MTART) is found in segment E1MARCM.
  • Copy the required fields to your Message Type.

 

2. Define the Filtering Fields in BD59 (If is needed)

  • Go to Transaction BD59.
  • Add Custom Message Type (e.g., ZMATMAS01).
  • Add your Object types for the Message.

3. Configure the Filters in BD64.

  • Go to Transaction BD64 (Distribution Model).
  • Select the Message Type (e.g., ZMATMAS01).

  • Expand Filter Group → Select Material Type and Plant.

  • Click Change Mode and add filter values (e.g., MTART = HALB, ROH; WERKS = W101).

  • If you want to go with standard message type use like this.

  • Save and activate the configuration.                                                                                                 

4.Perform Testing in BD10 & WE02

  • Positive Testing

 Test: Send a material matching the filter criteria.

                                  

  • Material having the filtering value Material type and Plant.
  • Go to BD10, enter Material Number & Message Type.

  • You will get a pop-up message for the confirmation that the message has been send.

  • Check WE02 to confirm IDoc generation.

  • Positive testing is over as the result we can see the IDoc have been send outside to other system.
  • Negative Testing

 Test: Send a material mismatch the filter criteria

                       

  • Go to BD10, enter Material Number & Message Type.

  • You will get a pop-up message for the confirmation that the message has been send but in this the communication will be 0.

  • Check WE02 to confirm IDoc generation.

  • No IDocs were Generated at for the material.                                                                                                                                                                                          

 So, the Negative Testing is also completed.

Conclusion

Filtering IDocs in BD64 ensures that only relevant data is sent, improving performance and reducing unnecessary processing. However, standard SAP behaviour might still generate IDocs under certain conditions.

entry.title: SAP Data & Analytics Strategy: Current insights and future prospects
entry.content:
The SAP Business Technology Platform is an innovation platform that has been specially optimized for SAP applications in the cloud. It enables companies to extend and personalize SAP applications and to integrate and network entire IT landscapes. By bringing together data and analytics, artificial intelligence (AI), application development, automation and integration in a unified environment, SAP BTP helps companies to connect processes, make informed decisions and continuously drive innovation.

SAP BTP

A central area is data and analytics, which supports companies in strategically utilizing their data and making data-based decisions.

Data and analytics in SAP BTP focuses on:

  1. Data management: The platform provides functions for integrating, processing and organizing data from different sources. This enables a central database that is reliable and consistent.

 

  1. Analysis functions: With powerful analysis tools, companies can evaluate data in real time. This allows trends to be identified, predictions to be made and decision-making processes to be optimized.

 

  1. Integration of SAP and third-party data: SAP BTP combines data from SAP applications such as ERP and CRM with data from other sources. This promotes a holistic view of business processes.

 

  1. Scalability and security: The platform offers flexible and scalable data solutions that are tailored to the requirements of each company. It also ensures the highest security standards to protect sensitive data.

This area enables companies to use their data as a strategic advantage by creating a basis for innovation, increased efficiency and competitive advantages.

Integrated Data & Analytics Portfolio

With SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Datasphere and SAP HANA Cloud, SAP offers three powerful solutions for data analysis, management and storage.

SAP Analytics Cloud is a comprehensive solution for analyses and planning scenarios. It enables companies to exploit the full potential of their data sources and maximize investments in business-critical applications. By integrating generative AI, reports can be automated, hidden insights gained and business plans developed. It also supports the consolidation of financial, supply chain and operational planning into one centralized solution.

SAP Datasphere gives data professionals seamless and scalable access to business-critical enterprise data. It provides reliable data by automatically reusing semantic definitions and associations from SAP applications. It also harmonizes heterogeneous data in a semantic business model and provides comprehensive access to all data in hybrid and cloud environments, regardless of where it is stored.

SAP HANA Cloud is SAP's database-as-a-service solution that combines powerful in-memory database technology with flexible data management capabilities. It enables companies to process, analyze and store large amounts of data in real time, supporting fast and informed decision-making. It also offers scalability and integration with other SAP and third-party applications to ensure a seamless data landscape.

These three products work seamlessly together to enable companies to implement a comprehensive data strategy, from data storage and management to analysis and planning.

SAP Analytics Cloud is a comprehensive analytics platform!

SAP Analytics Cloud: A platform for all requirements

Our last webinar “SAP Data & Analytics Strategy Update”, held jointly with SAP, focused on the SAP Analytics Cloud and its new functions. This platform has established itself as an indispensable tool for reporting, dashboarding and planning. Here is an overview of the most important new features:

The Data Analyzer offers a user-friendly option for ad-hoc analyses and rapid report generation. Users can gain detailed insights into their data without any major technical effort.

Interactive dashboards allow companies to visualize their data dynamically. Filter options and links enable flexible use of the information and promote collaboration.

The mobile usability of the SAP Analytics Cloud ensures that information is available anytime and anywhere. Whether on a smartphone or tablet, users are always able to act.

Another highlight are the powerful custom widgets, which enable companies to create individual visualizations and functions. With this flexibility, specific requirements can be implemented efficiently

 

A central component of the strategy presented is the seamless integration of SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere. This combination enables companies to optimize their planning and analysis processes.

Process flow and required steps

Seamless Planning with SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud

SAP is currently driving forward the integration between SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere with the introduction of "Seamless Planning". This new function makes it possible to store SAC data models directly in SAP Datasphere, which reduces the data inventory and enables real-time use as well as extended processing and expansion scenarios. The model structure and metadata will continue to be defined in SAC, but modelers can choose SAP Datasphere as the storage location when creating new models or public dimension tables. This promotes Seamless Planning and analysis on a simplified, centralized and managed data platform. All in all, a promising - and long-awaited - integrated solution that perfectly demonstrates the power of BTP.

The process in detail

  1. Establish the connection: SAC and SAP Datasphere are connected to each other.
  2. Create model: New planning models can be created directly in SAP Datasphere.
  3. Use integration: Planning data from SAC can be integrated into SAP Datasphere workflows.
  4. Check access rights: Roles and authorizations are controlled to ensure smooth collaboration.

With this integration, companies benefit from a uniform database that enables consistent planning and analysis. This not only promotes efficiency, but also decision-making quality.

 

Roadmap: The road to Seamless Planning

The roadmap for Seamless Planning presented here provides a clear outlook for future developments:

  • Q4 2024: Controlled release of first functions.

 

  • Q1 2025: General availability with extended range of functions.

The general availability of Seamless Planning is planned for the first quarter of release 2025. Prerequisites for use include both the SAC and SAP Datasphere being hosted in the same data center and using the same identity provider. In addition, the respective tenants must be connected to each other in a 1:1 relationship.

With Seamless Planning, companies can accelerate their planning processes, improve collaboration between different business units and make informed decisions based on harmonized and semantically enriched data. Future enhancements aim to further strengthen integration and provide additional functionality to realize the full potential of this powerful combination. Planned enhancements include the persistence of facts and master data and the use of planning data in SAP Datasphere workflows. These steps pave the way for even deeper integration and extended functionality.

Reference architecture based on SAP Datasphere

Data strategy of the future

Another important point is the reference architecture, which integrates SAP Datasphere as a central building block. By connecting source systems such as S/4HANA and ECC with external data sources, this architecture provides a flexible basis for data analysis.

The combination of replication and virtual access ensures that data is available both quickly and securely. This helps companies to implement a scalable and future-proof data strategy.

Rethinking data management

The updates clearly show how SAP is creating the basis for modern data management through continuous innovation. The SAP Analytics Cloud has established itself as a powerful platform that offers great added value through customer-specific adaptations. Seamless Planning and integration with SAP Datasphere ensure that companies can optimize their planning and analysis processes.

The future of SAP Data & Analytics promises further exciting developments and we at PCS beratungscontor remain a reliable partner for tapping into this potential.

entry.title: SAP SD Variant Pricing: Configuration and Implementation
entry.content:
Variant pricing in SAP SD provides a flexible pricing mechanism for configurable materials, allowing price determination based on material characteristics. This article outlines the configuration steps required to implement variant pricing effectively.

Advantages and Disadvantages of Variant Pricing

Advantages:

Enables dynamic pricing based on material variant configuration.

Reduces the effort needed to maintain existing variant pricing conditions.

 

Disadvantages:

Applicable only to configurable materials.

Requires a more complex setup compared to standard pricing.

 

Variant pricing is only applicable to configurable materials. The material must have a type of KMAT or a custom type derived from KMAT. Options of KMAT material type can be checked from OMS2.

 

 

You can check the material type via:

  • MARA-MTART field in the MARA table.
  • MM03 (Material Master) → Basic Data tab → Info button.

 

 

Also 0002 (Configuration) general item category group should be set for configurable materials.

 

“Material is configurable” checkbox should be set in MM01 → Basic Data 2.

 

The characteristic values those determines variant prices, can be set directly or copied from another characteristic (for example a characteristic determines component for production order). In this article, configuration of second scenario (Values are copied from another characteristic’s values) is shown.

 

Steps of Variant Pricing Configuration:

  • Create Characteristics in CT04

Use Transaction Code (Tcode) CT04 to create a characteristic.

Assign characteristic values matching the values from the referenced characteristic.

 

 

Under the Additional Data tab, set table name as SDCOM (SD and Variant Configuration Communication), field Name as VKOND (Variant Condition)

If values of characteristic are copied from another characteristic, check the “Not Ready for Input” option.

 

 

  • Create a Variant Class in CL01

Use Tcode CL01 to create a Class Type = 300 (Variants).

A variant class is created from CL01 by selecting 300 (variants) class type.

 

 

Assign the previously created characteristics to the class at “Char.” section.

Save the class.

 

 

  • Assign the Variant Class to the Material

Use Tcode MM02 and navigate to the Classification section and assign the created variant class.

Note: Alternatively, classes can also be assigned directly to material types in OMS2.

 

 

  • Define Dependency in CU01

The rules of the characteristic values ​​to be selected and their copying to other values ​​are determined through the dependencies.

Use Tcode CU01 to create a dependency.

Set Dependency Type as “Procedure”.

 

Define a dependency rule in the Dependency Editor to copy characteristic values to pricing characteristics. Dependency rule can be set from “Dependency editor” as shown in the image.

Save the dependency.

 

  • Create a Configuration Profile in CU41

Use Tcode CU41 to create a configuration profile for the material.

Double-click the profile.

 

 

Navigate to Dependency Assignments by clicking the button that marked in image.

 

 

Assign the previously created dependency.

 

Save the configuration profile.

 

  • Define Pricing Characteristics in VK30

Add pricing characteristic values to Variant Fields.

Ensure that these values align with the pricing structure.

 

 

 

  • Configure Pricing Procedure and Condition Records

 

Use V/03, V/04 to create or modify a condition table including variant (VARCOND) field.

Assign condition table to access sequence from V/07 by adding the new condition table to access.

 

 

Create a variant condition type from V/06.

Assign the new Access Sequence to this condition type.

 

 

Check the “Variant Condition” checkbox.

 

 

 

Add the variant condition to the pricing procedure from V/08.

 

 

Use VK11 to create condition records ensuring that, variant values match the pricing characteristic’s values and the values in VK30.

 

 

 

  • Testing the Pricing in Sales Order (VA01)

Create a sales order using VA01.

Assign characteristic values in Characteristic Value Assignment screen reaching by the button marked in the image belove.

 

 

 

After setting characteristic values, verify the price determination process in the conditions tab.

Confirm that pricing is correctly applied based on selected variant characteristics.

entry.title: Scaffold a SAP CAP App with CAP Builder
entry.content:
SAP Build Code and Joule’s capabilities have made me reflect on the relevance of past projects like App Templater and CAPGEN. These tools were designed to model and generate SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming) Node applications and services, but with AI tools are these still relevant?

One of the aspects I love about SAP CAP is its open-source stack. However, the tools such as SAP Build Code with Joule are not always easily accessible to everyone, leading to frustration. To address this, I decided to experiment by integrating OpenAI’s capabilities into App Templater, and the results were better than I could have hoped for. This inspired me to merge App Templater and CAPGEN into a single, more powerful Open Source application: CAP Builder.

Introducing CAP Builder

CAP Builder is now available on GitHub and provides a significantly improved user experience. Here are some of the key features:

  • AI Project Creation*: Generate an SAP CAP project using a simple text prompt.
  • AI Data Generation*: Automatically create relevant sample data for your application.
  • Code Generation & Download: Export a ZIP file containing all generated code, including a Fiori Elements application.
  • Live Preview: Instantly preview your application to see how it works in real-time.
  • Template File Export: Download and share your generated template file with others.

 As you will see in the demo video, starting with a text prompt, I modeled an application, generated test data, viewed a preview, made some modifications, reviewed the preview, and downloaded the source code. All in 8 minutes!

Try CAP Builder Today!

I encourage you to check out the demo video and visit the project repository to give CAP Builder a try. Your feedback and contributions are welcome, so let me know what you think and how we can improve this tool together!

*You will need an Open AI API key and credits to use the AI features.

entry.title: SAP Clean Core Development :: Managed vs. Unmanaged RAP Business Objects - architectural differences
entry.content:
The ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) offers two primary approaches for implementing business objects (BOs): managed and unmanaged. These approaches differ in how transactional logic, CRUD operations, and persistence management are handled. Below is a detailed comparison, including practical examples.

Core Definitions

Managed BO :

Delegates transactional logic to the RAP framework. The framework automatically handles CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) , Locking and draft handling , Interaction and save phases (transaction buffer management) , Developers focus on business logic (validations, determinations, actions) rather than technical implementation.

Unmanaged RAP Business Object:

An unmanaged BO requires developers to manually implement transactional logic in ABAP classes. This approach is used when:

  • Integrating legacy code (e.g., BAPIs, function modules).
  • Requiring full control over database operations.
  • Handling complex cross-system interactions.

Key Differences  Managed BO  Vs Unmanaged BO:

  1. CRUD Implementation Automatic via RAP framework Vs Manual coding in behavior implementation classes
  2. Locking :  Auto-managed Vs Manual implementation via ENQUEUE/DEQUEUE
  3. Draft Handling : Built-in Vs Must be manually coded
  4. Transaction Buffering : Managed by framework Vs Custom buffer handling required
  5. Use Case : Greenfield projects Vs Brownfield projects with existing code
  6. Code Overhead : Minimal (focus on business logic) Vs High (full control over persistence)

Implementation Example -  Employee Management System ::

Scenario 1: Managed BO

Behavior Definition (ZBP_I_EMPLOYEE_M):

 

abap
managed implementation in class zbp_i_employee_m unique; 
define behavior for ZI_EMPLOYEE_M 
{ 
  create; 
  update; 
  delete; 
  draft table ZDRAFT_EMP; 
}

 

Key Features:

  • RAP runtime automatically persists data to table ZEMPLOYEE.
  • Drafts stored in ZDRAFT_EMP with no custom code.
  • Locking managed via ETag in OData requests.

Scenario 2: Unmanaged BO

Behavior Definition (ZBP_I_EMPLOYEE_U):

 

abap unmanaged implementation in class zbp_i_employee_u unique; 
define behavior for ZI_EMPLOYEE_U 
{ 
  create; 
  update; 
  delete; 
}

 

Behavior Implementation (zbp_i_employee_u):

 

abap 
CLASS zbp_i_employee_u DEFINITION PUBLIC ABSTRACT FINAL FOR BEHAVIOR OF zi_employee_u. 
  PUBLIC SECTION. 
    METHODS create FOR MODIFY 
      IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE Employee. 
    METHODS update FOR MODIFY 
      IMPORTING entities FOR UPDATE Employee. 
    METHODS delete FOR MODIFY 
      IMPORTING keys FOR DELETE Employee. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS zbp_i_employee_u IMPLEMENTATION. 
  METHOD create. 
    DATA: lt_emp TYPE TABLE OF zemployee. 
    LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_emp). 
      INSERT VALUE #( 
        emp_id = ls_emp-EmpId 
        name = ls_emp-Name 
        dept = ls_emp-Dept 
      ) INTO TABLE lt_emp. 
    ENDLOOP. 
    INSERT zemployee FROM TABLE _emp. 
  ENDMETHOD. 


  METHOD update. 
    " Custom logic to update records in zemployee 
  ENDMETHOD. 

  METHOD delete. 
    " Custom logic to delete records from zemployee 
  ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

 

Key Features:

  • Manual SQL operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on table ZEMPLOYEE.
  • No auto-draft handling; requires custom tables and logic.
  • Locks must be explicitly managed via ENQUEUE_ZEMPLOYEE.

Transaction Flow Comparison - Managed BO Transaction Flow:

  1. Interaction Phase:
    • RAP buffers changes in the transaction buffer.
    • Validations/determinations execute via framework triggers.
  2. Save Phase:
    • Framework persists buffer to database in a single LUW.
    • Automatic optimistic locking via ETag.

Transaction Flow Comparison - Unmanaged BO Transaction Flow:

  1. Interaction Phase:
    • Custom code directly modifies the database (unless buffered manually).
  2. Save Phase:
    • Requires explicit COMMIT WORK or rollback handling.
    • Manual lock management using ENQUEUE/DEQUEUE.

When to Use Each Approach:

Managed BO Use Cases:

  1. New Applications: No legacy code dependencies.
  2. Rapid Prototyping: Leverage out-of-the-box CRUD.
  3. Standard UI Apps: Fiori Elements integration via CDS annotations.

Unmanaged BO Use Cases:

  1. Legacy Integration: Wrap existing BAPIs/function modules.
    • Example: Migrate an HR payroll system using BAPI_EMPLOYEE_CREATE.
  2. Custom Persistence: Save data to non-standard tables or external systems.
  3. Complex Transactions: Multi-system commits requiring explicit control.

Hybrid Approach: Managed with Unmanaged Save

For partial control over persistence:

 

abap
define behavior for ZI_EMPLOYEE_HYBRID 
{ 
  create; 
  update; 
  delete; 
  persistent table zemployee 
  save always; 
} 
CRUD managed by RAP, but save method overridden:
abap
CLASS zbp_i_employee_hybrid DEFINITION. 
  PUBLIC SECTION. 
    METHODS save REDEFINITION. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS zbp_i_employee_hybrid IMPLEMENTATION. 
  METHOD save. 
    " Custom audit logging before saving 
    super->save( ). 
  ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

 

Managed BOs streamline development for greenfield projects by abstracting technical complexities. Unmanaged BOsoffer flexibility for integrating legacy systems but demand manual coding. Choose based on:

  • Project Type: New (managed) vs. Legacy (unmanaged).
  • Control Needs: Framework automation vs. custom logic.
  • Team Skills: ABAP for unmanaged vs. declarative CDS for managed.

For most SAP S/4HANA cloud projects, managed BOs are preferred. However, hybrid models (e.g., managed with unmanaged save) balance agility and customization for complex scenarios.

entry.title: Feasibility of OEE KPI in Digital Manufacturing
entry.content:
Dear Community,

Earlier, there was significant feasibility in viewing the OEE report as demonstrated in the referenced video . However, I am currently unable to access this functionality in SAP DM. Based on this, we have a few questions regarding the OEE report:

  • Is it possible to view the OEE KPI at the plant level / enterprise level? We tried using the Manage dashboard ,but the MDO are not clear enough to create this kind of report. If so, could you please provide guidance on how to access this report? 
  • How can we check the OEE KPI for a specific date range (e.g., 1 month or 3 months)? At the moment, we can only access data for the current day and the previous day through the standard OEE report. We would like to know how to access a broader range of historical data.
  • OEE KPI Visibility for Multiple Work Centers: When selecting 3-4 work centers, we require the OEE KPI at both the individual work center level and as a cumulative value across all selected work centers. It would be helpful to have the cumulative OEE KPI across multiple work centers in one consolidated report.

Note: We are able to see OEE report at the individual work center level.

Kind Regards,
Utkarsh Raj

SAP Digital Manufacturing

entry.title: Creating Custom BRF+ Form from Scratch
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community, 

my current task is to create a new form in our S4 Hana System based on SAPs new output management BRF+. I've done the following steps up to this point:

  • created a new BRF+ Application based on OPD_EF_PURCHASE_ORDER in brf+ TA
  • created a new SEGW Service to use with the Form
  • created custom database tables for positions and head data
  • created cds views to connect head and positions table with uuids which are automatically determined when creating a new entry

I'm currently stuck and don't really know how to continue. I think I need to create a Output Control Application Object for the new form, but I can't find how to create one. 

If anyone knows the necessary steps to continue I would really appreciate a comment. Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Are You Ready for the Mandatory Shift to E-invoicing in Belgium?
entry.content:
The world of invoicing is evolving, and e-invoicing is no longer just a trend, it is a legal requirement that businesses must follow.  Also, in Belgium the commitment to implement e-invoicing broadly has become real.  The law for mandatory domestic B2B e-invoicing in Belgium was published in the Belgian Official Journal on February 20, 2024 and will be effective on January 1, 2026.

The shift to mandatory e-invoicing is not just about business efficiency anymore, it is about adhering to stringent tax regulations and ensuring that your invoicing processes are compliant with legislative requirements.  This significant change presents an urgent need for businesses to ensure their processes are well-prepared for the upcoming changes and to define a strong compliance strategy with the right tools.

Why It’s Time to Act Now: The Transition to E-invoicing

The shift from traditional invoicing methods to e-invoicing is not simply a matter of adopting a more efficient tool, it is a matter of staying compliant with tax requirements.  It is essential for companies to realize that digitalization of invoices is no longer optional, and getting ahead of this change will save both time and resources in the long run.  While some companies may have used EDI (Electronic Data Interchange) to enhance business efficiency in the past, e-invoicing is now the legal expectation.

The shift to e-invoicing mandates transcends borders.  A lot of countries already adopted e-invoicing legislation.  Other countries are now taking significant steps forward to the implementation of e-invoicing mandates.  In Belgium, for example, the B2B e-invoicing mandate will enter into force on January 1, 2026.  Many other countries will follow this trend, such as France, Germany, etc.  This means that companies need a solution that can scale to meet global compliance requirements.

How SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Helps You Stay Compliant Globally

The core objective is to make sure businesses are aligned with the requirements set out by governments for electronic invoicing.

This is where SAP Document and Reporting Compliance comes into play.  SAP Document and Reporting Compliance is designed to help businesses navigate the complexities of e-invoicing and ensure seamless compliance across multiple jurisdictions.  With this solution companies can easily send and receive e-invoices, managing this entire process end-to-end with minimal effort.  The system integrates directly into your existing SAP landscape, making the transition as smooth as possible.

With SAP Document and Reporting Compliance there is a global approach for coping with e-invoicing mandates.  Our solution already supports over 55 countries, and many more countries are on the development roadmap, such as Belgium, France, Germany, etc.  This means whether you’re operating in the EU, in Latin America, or beyond, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance  help you to meeting local e-invoicing requirements, no matter where you do business.

Next to the support of e-invoicing mandates, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance also manages regulatory reporting requirements from many countries worldwide.  For Belgium, for instance, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance supports the filing of the VAT returns (periodic VAT return, intra-Community statement and annual sales list), the balance of payments reporting, etc.

By providing this extensive global coverage, SAP ensures your company is always up to date with the latest regulatory requirements.  The solution is built with flexibility in mind, allowing it to handle various invoicing scenarios across different regions.

In addition, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance provides robust features for data validation, error handling, and reporting, further increasing automation and efficiency in your invoicing processes.  The system’s end-to-end capabilities make it the ideal solution for businesses looking to minimize manual work while ensuring they meet complex legal requirements.

Stay Up to Date with Regulatory Change Manager App of SAP

Regulatory requirements are continuously evolving, and it is essential for businesses to stay on top of these changes.  Through the Regulatory Change Manager app (regulatory change manager) you can stay updated with SAP’s analysis of how these changes impact their solutions.

Conclusion: Don’t Wait — Get Ready for the Future of E-invoicing Today

As increasing digitalization in tax administration is reshaping conventional practices and e-invoicing regulations are constantly evolving, now is the time to act.  It is time to equip your company with the right tools and ensure you are fully prepared for the mandatory shift to e-invoicing.  Let SAP Document and Reporting Compliance guide you every step of the way, so you can focus on growing your business, not worrying about compliance.

 

Stay tuned for more in the coming weeks and share any questions you may have in the comments section below!

 

If you want to learn about the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance in your country and engage with SAP experts, join your regional collaboration group.

Check out the SAP Community for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance.

 

You can find more details on the e-invocing in our dedicated blogs:

  • Revolutionizing Taxation: Navigating VAT in the Digital Age (ViDA)
  • SAP Document and Reporting Compliance: Cloud or On-Premise? Not an “either or” option, but a streamlined solution for electronic compliance!
  • I received an electronic supplier invoice, what now?
