entry.title: 🤖 AI in SAP: Supercharging Digital Transformation Like Never Before!
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) has become a game-changer in SAP implementations, revolutionizing enterprise resource planning (ERP), decision-making, and operational efficiency. Over the past decade, SAP’s AI capabilities have evolved from basic automation to sophisticated predictive analytics, intelligent automation, and generative AI innovations.

By 2025, SAP’s AI-driven solutions—including SAP Business AI, SAP AI Core, and generative AI toolkits—are helping organizations optimize supply chains, personalize customer interactions, and streamline HR processes with unparalleled precision. These advancements are not just incremental; they represent a fundamental shift in how businesses leverage data, automate workflows, and respond to ever-changing market dynamics.

From predictive maintenance in manufacturing to AI-enhanced financial forecasting, SAP’s AI innovations are delivering tangible improvements in cost savings, operational agility, and strategic foresight. Let’s explore the key advancements and their impact on the enterprise landscape.

 

📈The Evolution of SAP’s AI Strategy

SAP’s AI journey started with simple task automation but has since evolved into a comprehensive AI-driven enterprise solution. Initially, AI was used to reduce manual data entry and streamline repetitive processes. Today, thanks to machine learning (ML) and natural language processing (NLP), AI is embedded across SAP’s ecosystem, transforming business processes at every level.

🔄From Automation to Predictive Intelligence

SAP’s AI evolution has moved from rule-based automation to predictive and generative AI.

  • SAP Intelligent Robotic Process Automation (RPA) now automates complex workflows, like invoice processing and supplier onboarding, reducing manual effort by up to 70%.
  • SAP AI Core & SAP Data Intelligence enable organizations to build custom AI models that predict equipment failures, optimize inventory, and personalize customer experiences.

🌍Strategic Integration with SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)

SAP BTP acts as the backbone for AI deployments, seamlessly integrating AI across SAP and third-party applications. This is crucial for hybrid environments, where legacy SAP ECC systems coexist with S/4HANA and other cloud platforms.

 

🔑Core Technologies Powering SAP AI

SAP’s AI ecosystem is built on several key technologies that enhance automation, data processing, and decision-making. Here are the core components:

⚙️SAP AI Core & AI Launchpad

  • A scalable AI engine that enables businesses to train, deploy, and monitor machine learning (ML) models.
  • Used in industries like retail for demand forecasting by analyzing historical sales, weather, and social media trends.

📊SAP Data Intelligence

  • Orchestrates data pipelines across disparate sources, ensuring AI models receive clean, contextualized data.
  • Essential for industries like pharmaceuticals, where real-time trial data accelerates drug development.

💡SAP Business AI

  • Infuses AI-driven insights across SAP applications like SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, and SAP Customer Experience.
  • Helps automate repetitive tasks, optimize decision-making, and enhance user experiences with AI-powered recommendations.

📈SAP HANA with AI & Predictive Analytics

  • Provides real-time analytics, ML capabilities, and in-memory processing to drive intelligent business decisions.
  • Enables predictive maintenance in manufacturing and fraud detection in finance.

🤖SAP Intelligent RPA & Conversational AI

  • SAP Intelligent RPA automates end-to-end business processes like purchase orders and employee onboarding.
  • SAP Conversational AI powers AI-driven chatbots for seamless customer service and employee interactions.

🧠 SAP AI Agents (Upcoming)

  • Future AI-powered agents focused on sales, supply chain optimization, pricing strategies, and inventory management.

🔧SAP AI Foundation on Business Technology Platform (BTP)

  • A unified platform for AI-driven application development, automation, and data management.
  • Supports embedding AI models into SAP and third-party applications.

 

🏗️ How Businesses Are Integrating SAP Business AI

✅New SAP Implementations

Companies deploying SAP S/4HANA can integrate AI into core processes:

  • Manufacturers use AI for predictive maintenance, analyzing machine sensor data to prevent failures.

🔄Modernizing Legacy Systems

  • Businesses upgrading from SAP ECC to S/4HANA leverage AI for real-time analytics and decision-making.
  • A European retailer used SAP Business AI to analyze decades of sales data and optimize regional inventory strategies.

☁️Hybrid & Multi-Cloud Environments

  • SAP Business AI enables cross-platform AI-driven insights.
  • A healthcare provider unified SAP SuccessFactors and third-party HR software to enhance talent management analytics.

 

🚀Real-World AI Applications in SAP

🔍Predictive Analytics in Supply Chain Management

AI-driven insights help companies avoid disruptions by analyzing:

  • Supplier lead times
  • Geopolitical risks
  • Market demand shifts

During the 2024 Suez Canal crisis, SAP AI helped automotive manufacturers reduce delivery delays by 40% by dynamically adjusting supply routes.

🏢AI-Driven HR with SAP SuccessFactors

  • AI removes biases in hiring & promotions.
  • AI-driven career development plans led to a 30% increase in employee retention for a global corporation.

🛍️ Hyper-Personalized Customer Experiences

  • SAP AI correlates purchase history with social media trends to improve product recommendations.
  • Consumer goods companies have seen conversion rates jump by 25% with AI-driven marketing campaigns.

⚠️Challenges & Considerations for AI Adoption

🔹Align AI with Business Goals – Deploy AI strategically in high-impact areas like fraud detection and supply chain resilience.

🔹Ensure Data Readiness – Migrating to SAP HANA and cleansing legacy data are key for successful AI adoption.

🔹Ethical AI & Compliance – SAP implements Explainable AI (XAI) and AI governance frameworks to meet EU AI Act standards.

🚀The Future of AI in SAP

The next wave of SAP AI advancements will focus on:

  • Generative AI for self-optimizing workflows
  • Quantum computing for ultra-fast data processing
  • More accessible AI tools for business users

SAP’s AI transformation is more than just innovation—it’s redefining how enterprises plan, operate, and compete in a digital world.

Organizations that strategically integrate SAP Business AI will lead the next era of enterprise intelligence and automation. The question is: Is your business AI-ready? 🚀

 

References:

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-s-pioneering-leap-in-artificial-intelligence-transforming-enterprise/ba-p/13987619

  • https://technologymagazine.com/articles/how-sap-deploys-ai-across-enterprise-support-systems

  • https://www.precisely.com/blog/sap-automation/ai-powered-digital-transformation-get-your-data-and-ai-ready

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning-blogs/5-major-generative-ai-innovations-unveiled-at-sap-teched-2024/ba-p/13898803

  • https://www.whitehallresources.com/2024/05/ai-for-sap/

  • https://digital.neweratech.com/articles/ai-in-sap-leading-digital-transformation-with-intelligent-technologies

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/how-sap-s-generative-ai-architecture-redefines-business-applications/ba-p/13580679

  • https://procurementmag.com/technology-and-ai/sap-unveils-ai-innovations-to-mitigate-supply-chain-risks

  • https://customerland.net/sap-unveils-groundbreaking-ai-innovations-at-sap-sapphire-2024/

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/generative-ai-with-sap-part-1-introduction-to-sap-business-ai/ba-p/13792501

  • https://www.oxya.com/blog/integrate-sap-business-ai-into-your-enterprise/

  • https://www.suretysystems.com/insights/revolutionizing-enterprises-how-sap-ai-transforms-business-operations/

entry.title: End-to-End Guide: Deploying a Fiori App – From BTP Destination Creation to Public Cloud Deployment
entry.content:
Overview

Deploying an application in the public cloud requires a structured approach involving multiple configurations and integrations. This guide provides a detailed, step-by-step process to ensure a smooth deployment. The key steps include setting up destination connections, creating communication systems, building the app in Business Application Studio (BAS), deploying it, and configuring it in ABAP. This document also highlights important screenshot points to help visualize each step.

Step 1: Set Up the Destination Connection

  1. Open BTP Cockpit and Login: Access the BTP cockpit and log in to your account.
  2. Navigate to Sub-Account: Select and go to the relevant sub-account.
  3. Go to Destinations: Navigate to the "Destinations" section and create a new destination.
  4. Configure Destination Settings:
    • URL: Copy the hostname URL from the Communication Systems app and paste it into the destination URL field. Make sure that to use the URL of the development client to build and deploy an application. If the URL of customizing client is used, then it will lead to issues while deploying application.
    • Authentication Type: Use SAMLAssertion.
    • Audience: Retrieve the OAuth 2.0 SAML2 Audience from the technical data section in the Communication Systems app.

       

    • AuthnContextClassRef: Set it to urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:PreviousSession.
    • HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
    • HTML5.Timeout: 60000
    • nameIdFormat: urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress
    • sap-client: Specify your client (e.g., 100).
    • WebIDEEnabled: true
    • WebIDESystem: Specify your system (e.g., KOE).
    • WebIDEUsage: dev_abap, odata_abap, bsp_execute_abap, ui5_execute_abap.

       

  5. Download Trust File: Click on "Download Trust" to save the trust configuration locally.

Step 2: Create a Communication System in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

  1. Access the Communication Systems App: Open the app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

     

  2. Create a New Communication System:
    • Specify the system ID as BAS_<Subdomain Name> and provide a meaningful system name.

       

  3. Set Technical Data:
    • Go to the "Technical Data" section and select the checkbox for "Inbound Only".

       

    • Go to the identity provider and turn the SAML Bearer Assertion provider.

       

  4. Configure Identity Provider:
    • Enable the "SAML Bearer Assertion" provider.
    • Upload the trust file downloaded in the previous step.
    • After uploading the document fill the above SAML Bearer Issuer field by coping the address from the Signing certificate Subject from the “cfapps….” to till the last.

Step 3: Create the Application in BAS

  1. Open BAS: Access the SAP Business Application Studio.
  2. Create Application Using Template:
    • Select the "SAP Fiori Generator" template.
  3. Configure Application:
    • Select the template as "List Report Page" or required template.

       

    • Choose the data source, system, and OData service.

       

    • Select the main entity of the service.

       

  4. Add Project Attributes:
    • Provide details like project name, namespace, and deployment configurations.

       

       
  5. Add Deployment Configuration:
    • Add the required details for deployment.

       

  6. Configure Fiori Launchpad:
    • Add the required details for the launchpad configuration.

       

  7. Finalize and Create Project: Click "Next" and create the project.

Step 4: Deploy the App from BAS

  1. Open Integrated Terminal:
    • Right-click on the project folder and open the terminal.
  2. Run Deployment Command:
    • Execute "npm run deploy" in the terminal.

       

    • Verify details and confirm deployment by typing y. 
  3. Verify Deployment: Ensure the deployment completes successfully.

     

  4. After deploying the application, if you see the preview of the application, you will get a 404 error. That is because the deployed app should be configured and published in IAM apps in Eclipse.

Step 5: Configure the App in ABAP (Eclipse)

  1. Open Eclipse: Launch Eclipse and open the package used for deployment.
  2. Create IAM App:
    • Right-click on the package and create an IAM app.

       

    • Provide name, description, and set Application Type to "External App".
    • Assign a transport request (TR) and finish the setup.

       

  3. Add Fiori Launchpad App ID:
    • Locate the app ID under the package: Fiori User Interface > Launchpad App Descriptor Items.
    • Add this ID to the IAM app configuration.

       

  4. Create Business Catalog:
    • Create and assign the app to a new business catalog.

       

    • Click on next and select the TR and click on next.
    • Then the dialog opens for creating the Business catalog app assignment, set the name and description and select next and choose the TR.

       

  5. Publish the Catalog and App:
    • Publish the business catalog locally.
    • Publish the IAM app. 
  6. Check the preview of the application form the deployed link.

Step 6: Assign the App to Users

  1. Verify Published IAM App and Catalog:
    • Open the "Display IAM App" to check the published app.

       

    • Verify the catalog in the "Business Catalogs" app.

       

  2. Create Spaces and Pages:
    • Define spaces and pages in the Fiori launchpad.
    • Assign the app to a role and add users to the role.
  3. Verify User Access: Ensure the app is available on the user’s home screen.

Summary

This guide walks you through deploying an SAP Fiori app in the public cloud, covering destination setup, BAS development, deployment, and ABAP configuration. Step-by-step instructions with screenshot placeholders ensure clarity. Following this process minimizes errors for a smooth deployment.

entry.title: Prototype Design Pattern in SAP OOABAP
entry.content:
Prototype Design Pattern in SAP OOABAP

The Prototype Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that allows cloning existing objects without requiring their exact class to be specified. This is useful when object creation is expensive, and duplicating an existing object is more efficient.

When to Use the Prototype Pattern?

  • When object creation is costly (e.g., involves database queries or complex calculations).
  • When you need copying/cloning of objects with modified attributes.
  • When creating objects dynamically at runtime.

Implementation of Prototype Pattern in SAP OOABAP

We implement the Prototype Pattern in ABAP using the Clone Method.

Step 1: Define an Interface for Cloning

We create an interface if_prototype that enforces cloning functionality.

 

INTERFACE if_prototype.
  METHODS: clone RETURNING VALUE(ro_clone) TYPE REF TO if_prototype.
ENDINTERFACE.

 

Step 2: Create a Concrete Class That Implements Cloning

The class lcl_product represents a product with a name and price. It implements the clone method.

 

CLASS lcl_product DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_prototype.
    DATA: product_name TYPE string,
          product_price TYPE p DECIMALS 2.
    METHODS:
      constructor IMPORTING iv_name TYPE string iv_price TYPE p,
      display_product.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS lcl_product IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD constructor.
    me->product_name = iv_name.
    me->product_price = iv_price.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD display_product.
    WRITE: / 'Product:', product_name, 'Price:', product_price.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_prototype~clone.
    DATA(lo_clone) = NEW lcl_product( iv_name = me->product_name iv_price = me->product_price ).
    ro_clone = lo_clone.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

Step 3: Use the Prototype Pattern

We create an instance of lcl_product, clone it, and modify its attributes.

 

DATA: lo_original TYPE REF TO lcl_product,
      lo_clone TYPE REF TO if_prototype.

" Create Original Object
CREATE OBJECT lo_original
  EXPORTING iv_name = 'Laptop'
            iv_price = 1000.
WRITE: / 'Original Product: '.
lo_original->display_product( ).

" Clone the Object
lo_clone = lo_original->if_prototype~ clone( ).

" Cast clone to lcl_product to modify attributes
DATA(lo_cloned_product) = CAST lcl_product( lo_clone ).
lo_cloned_product->product_price = 1200.

WRITE: / 'Cloned Product (Modified Price): '.
lo_cloned_product->display_product( ).

 

🔹Output

🔹Advantages of the Prototype Pattern in ABAP

✔ Efficient Object Creation: Reduces cost when creating multiple similar objects.
✔ Dynamic Object Modifications: You can create variations without modifying the original object.
✔ Encapsulation of Object Creation: Clients don’t need to know object creation logic.

This pattern is commonly used in SAP RAP & BOPF for handling object duplication scenarios.

entry.title: Internet Communication Manager Admin page issue in SAP
entry.content:
Sometime we are getting an issue where if a user/non-user browses to the below mentioned type of link, Where they on the network or public internet, they can see the below information without having to login, we need to get this restricted this, so this blog motive is how to restrict the information details.

Example:- https://apps.Name.gov.int/sap/admin/public/index.html  

                              Photo:-1 

How to fix this:-

There is a parameter that is allowing this information to be shown without having to sign in, and gives a sign in option in the top right and a message saying Monitoring Rights granted.

First, we make changes in icm/HTTP/admin_0 parameter. You can set the same in DEFAULT profile

Like show below:

                                  Photo:-2

Request you to set icm/HTTP/admin_0 as below:

                                    Photo:-3

Update the below mentioned new Value and Save and Restart the SMICM.

icm/HTTP/admin_0 = PREFIX=/sap/admin, DOCROOT=S:\usr\sap\SID\DVEBMGS00\data\icmandir/admin, AUTHFILE=S:\usr\sap\SID\SYS\global\security\data\icmauth.txt, ALLOWPUB=FALSE

Sometimes in RZ10 restricted the characters, which we put in that new parameter. Then we make change in server in Default parameter directory (usr/sap/SID/SYS/profile/default) and paste the below parameter. Like shown below and save.

icm/HTTP/admin_0 = PREFIX=/sap/admin,DOCROOT=S:\usr\sap\SID\DVEBMGS00\data\icmandir/admin, AUTHFILE=S:\usr\sap\SID\SYS\global\security\data\icmauth.txt, ALLOWPUB=FALSE

                                        Photo :-4

Moreover, after the make change in Parameter We need to take a restart of only ICM. Full system restart will not be needed. You can restart the ICM via SMICM T-code as well.

Jus log on SAP GUI and RUN SMICM>Administration>Exit Soft>Global

                                     Photo:-5

Moreover, refresh the system after some time SMICM is fixed and portal show the restriction and login message popped up.

                                  Photo:-6

In summary, we are getting an issue where if a user/non-user browses, where the network details is public,

this blog help to how to solve the issue.

Hope you enjoyed reading.

Conclusion-

We discussed how to solve the ICM issue on portal, and how to restrict it on portal with the parameter. 

Related information's-

https://help.sap.com/doc/saphelp_nw74/7.4.16/en-US/48/89061f33b11b5ae10000000a42189c/content.htm?no_cache=true

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_NETWEAVER_AS_ABAP_752/077f95b579a0474fbef5d520be10b680/5a915311b729421089f7706f9ee6771e.html

entry.title: Value Mapping in SAP Master Data governance MDG-M
entry.content:
Introduction:

In SAP, value mapping refers to the process of mapping and transforming values from one system or set of values to another within the context of master data. This is particularly important when you are integrating SAP MDG with other systems or consolidating data from multiple sources.

In this example, we will see how a value mapping can be configured in SAP MDG with respect to Material master field – Division (MARA-SPART)

Let us consider that we have a source system (MDG Hub) and material master in the source system gets replicated via IDOC to transactional SAP system. As per the requirement, the Division value 30 in the source system needs to be converted to value 35 when data gets syndicated out to the target system. 

Details:

Step 1: Identify the Global Data type for the field SPART. For this you can go to below path

MDGIMG – Central governance for Material - Maintain Fields for IDOC Value Mapping.

As given in the below screenshot, the global data type for field SPART is MDGCO_DIVISION_CODE

Step 2: Maintain Value Mapping for the global data type. Go to path

MDGIMG – Data Replication settings – Value Mapping – Maintain Value mapping

Select the Navigation button and start assigning the ‘Assign Code list’ and values

In this ‘Assign code list’ Overview screen – add a list ID/mapping ID for the list of values for which you needed the conversion to happen.

Assign the list of values in the ‘Define Value Mapping’ that needs to be converted. Internal Code value is the value that will be sent by source system and External code value is the value that will be received by the target system.

In this example, I have added only one entry as Division Code 30 from MDG needs to be converted to Code 35 in the transactional SAP system.

Step 3: Now go to the below path

MDGIMG – Data Replication settings – Value Mapping – Assign Code Lists to Elements and Systems

Add a new entry by updating the Global Data type, Business system (target system), list ID, List agency id and version id

(Note the Global Data type, List ID, List Agency ID and version ID to the be the same as added in the previous screen)

Step 4:

Now update/replicate a material with Division '30' from MDG HUB and you can see that the material gets replicated out with '35' for the business system configured in the value mapping settings.

 

Conclusion:

Thus, the MDG value mapping configuration helps us to integrate the MDG system with other downstream/upstream systems by mapping and transforming values as per downstream/upstream requirements

entry.title: Re: Need help with X015 and X115 PCRs
entry.content:
This additional query should have been made with a more appropriate "Question" since it concerns more Payroll Operation than a particular Payroll Calculation Rule.  Nevertheless, here is my answer:

Using t-code pe04, you can look at the documentation for the Payroll Operations, using the "i" icon 

for the Payroll Operation ADDNA *

and in your pcr, that translates as the RTE will only be transfered to the OT once, even if there are multiple occurrences of the WT in the Input Table (but the NUM and AMT will be added to each other).

As for Payroll Operation WGTYP=* 

Note that you can also select the highlighted portion of the text (in orange) to get more information on Operation WGTYP= , but it is useful only if you want to modify the WT.  In your case, WGTYP=* means that the WT is transferred to the OT as the same WT.

For any Payroll Operation, take the time to read the documentation on it, drilling down into it when multiple options are available, such as with Operation AMT (for example), and do not hesitate to test some of these options in your DEVelopment environment.

entry.title: Handle Asynchronous task in background job from a stateless UI application using RAP business object
entry.content:
Requirement: An application built using BAS on BTP for an RAP business object has a requirement of triggering an asynchronous task in the background when a button is clicked. Example: ( this example will be used to explain the implementation ) when the button is clicked, the user uploads an excel file with data and on click of OK, the data needs to be saved but as it is a large amount of data it will take some time, so the UI shouldn't be waiting for a response from the backend. 

Challenge: background processing framework has been introduced by SAP from Cloud 2311 version. Without this framework, it is not possible to trigger an asynchronous task from a stateless UI. 

Solution: To simulate similar functionality we can use existing ABAP artifacts like RFC and submitting program in background job.

Overview: In this example, I have implemented the following:

  • a new action with parameters is defined in the behavior definition. The key of the RAP object will hold the keys of the table that need to be changed. The new values to be updated are passed by the UI as parameters to the action

 

 

action (features : instance) upload_file  parameter ZSD_ABS_CONTRACTUAL_INDATA;

 

 

  • an RFC function module is created that accepts the data from the file in the form of an internal table
  • Above RFC function module is called from the RAP action implementation ( with the addition destination 'NONE' as otherwise commit cannot be done from the FM to schedule the job ). 

 

  METHOD upload_file.

    DATA: gt_return   TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zsds_soitem_message,
          lt_sch_data TYPE zsdt_soschdata.

    IF NOT keys[] IS INITIAL.
      LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_keys).
        APPEND INITIAL LINE TO lt_sch_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_sch_data>).
        <ls_sch_data>-vbeln = ls_keys-salesdocument.
        <ls_sch_data>-posnr = ls_keys-salesdocumentitem.
        <ls_sch_data>-del_dt = COND #( WHEN ls_keys-%param-newcontractualdt IS NOT INITIAL
                                       THEN ls_keys-%param-newcontractualdt ).
        <ls_sch_data>-email = cond #( WHEN ls_keys-%param-email IS NOT INITIAL
                                       THEN ls_keys-%param-email ).

      ENDLOOP.

      CALL FUNCTION 'ZSD_DEL_DATE_UPD_FILE' DESTINATION 'NONE'
        EXPORTING
          it_sch_data   = lt_sch_data
        EXCEPTIONS
          error_message = 99.

    ENDIF.

  ENDMETHOD.

 

 

  • The function module calls the 'JOB_OPEN' and 'JOB_CLOSE' function modules to submit another program via a background job.
  • The program runs asynchronously as a job and updates the data and also sends an email with the results to the email-id ( the parameter email in the previous screenshot )
  • Email functionality has been implemented so the user is informed of the success or failure of the task as the UI doesn't wait for a response and simply displays a message that the data will be updated in background.
  • The button and the popup that allows the user to upload an excel file:

 

 

Conclusion: The above approach can be used to call an asynchronous task from an RAP object for OP SAP versions. With SAP Cloud 2311, it will also be possible to update the UI once the asynchronous task is completed using event driven actions.

entry.title: Automated Undeployment and deployment of selective iflows during planned downtime
entry.content:
During planned system downtime or in case of emergency downtime, we require all iflows to be undeployed at the start of downtime and then redeploy them again when the downtime is over. This task usually is performed manually and requires a dedicated person to be available to perform the activity. There are blogs created by fellow community members which provide solution to deploy-undeploy all iflows. However, there are cases where all iflows or artifacts need not be undeployed. For eg: in case of S4HANA downtime, only iflows inbound to S4HANA need to be undeployed while outbound ones, value mapping, script collections etc. do not require to be undeployed.

This blog provides detailed steps and components that are required to manage only selective iflows during downtime.

Un Deployment Process

The first step of setting up this functionality is to identify the list of iflows which are needed to be undeployed during outage. 

This list can be maintained in an excel file or notepad file with each Iflow ID in a separate line.

(i) Create a simple iflow to write the iflow list to a datastore. This entry in datastore should be a one-time activity unless there is a change in the list of iflows to be deployed or undeployed.

(ii) Un Deployment Process

Create an iflow to initiate the undeployment process.

 

Firstly, we initialize a datastore (FailedIflowList) with blank payload for storing the list of iflows which fail to undeploy in later processing.

Now we load of list of iflows that need to be undeployed from Datastore (IflowList) that was loaded in the first step.

This iflow list will be stored in a property which will be used later for triggering email for the final status of undeployment.

Create an XSD like below which can be used for converting the CSV payload into XML format.

Use a csv to xml convert to convert the CSV payload into XML using the XSD created above.

Use General Splitter to split each Iflow entry into individual xml which will be passed to local integration process.

Now, local subprocess call is made to execute the undeployment process.

 

Fetch CSRF token from Cloud Integration URL using below logic

(a) Set x-csrf-token=fetch in header of the content modifier.

(b) use a Request-Reply to fetch the CSRF token by calling Cloud Integration URL 

Reset the split payload as body and then extract the iflow ID from xpath and set it as property.

Perform HTTP call to CPI endpoint URL as shown below to undeploy the iflow

Check if the undeployment call was successful

If successful, end the subprocess. Else, get the list of failed iflows from datastore (FailedIflowList) which was initialized earlier. For first record, the list will be empty but in further iterations, it might have some records. Now append this artifact id to the list and rewrite back to datastore.  

Once the control is passed to the main integration process, it checks whether the last record has been processed by checking for condition ${property.CamelSplitComplete} = 'true'

Once the last record from the iflow list has been processed, we read the datastore FailedIflowList if any entries are present. If no entry is found, a success email is sent to stakeholders notifying that the process is successful.

In case of failure, a process completion notification along with the list of failed iflows is sent to them so that they can take necessary action accordingly.

Deployment Process

The deployment process is almost identical to the undeployment process except the endpoint and method that we use for deploying the iflow.

Firstly, we initialize a datastore (FailedIflowList) with blank payload for storing the list of iflows which fail to undeploy in later processing.

Now we load of list of iflows that need to be undeployed from Datastore (IflowList)

This iflow list will be stored in a property which will be used later for triggering email for the final status of deployment.

Use the same xsd that we created in undeployment process for converting the CSV payload into XML format.

Use General Splitter to split each Iflow entry into individual xml which will be passed to local integration process.

Now, local subprocess call is made to execute the deployment process.

fetch CSRF token from Cloud Integration URL using below logic

(a) Set x-csrf-token=fetch in header of the content modifier.

(b) use a Request-Reply to fetch the CSRF token by calling Cloud Integration URL 

Set the response header set-cookie in an exchange property cookie.

set header cookie after formatting in groovy script as shown below

Reset the split payload as body and then extract the iflow ID from xpath and set it as property.

Perform HTTP call to CPI endpoint URL as shown below to deploy the iflow

Check if the deployment is successful.

If successful, end the subprocess. Else, get the list of failed iflows from datastore (FailedIflowList) which was initialized earlier. For first record, the list will be empty but in further iterations, it might have some records. Now append this artifact id to the list and rewrite back to datastore.

Once the control is passed to the main integration process, it checks whether the last record has been processed by checking for condition ${property.CamelSplitComplete} = 'true'

Once the last record from the iflow list has been processed, we read the datastore FailedIflowList if any entries are present. If no entry is found, a success email is sent to stakeholders notifying that the process is successful.

In case of failure, a process completion notification along with the list of failed iflows is sent to them so that they can take necessary action accordingly.

entry.title: Re: CCM - WB2R_SC - Partial Settlement
entry.content:
Hi @arindamsur, 

Thanks for your post. As explained via LinkedIn, your post doesn't have much information of the context you're facing but here are a few clarifications that might help address your issues:

1. In standard SAP CCM behavior, tax calculation is typically not required for delta accrual postings, unless you're operating in a specific country with mandatory tax requirements (e.g., I believe Italy is one of them).
Therefore, in most cases, the accounting document for delta accruals will not contain tax codes, and this is standard system behavior, not an error.
Since the system does not determine tax during accrual postings, the posting status will still show as green (posted successfully), even if tax codes are not present.

2. In standard configuration, Partial Settlement documents should post directly to accounting, unless you are using a two-step condition contract type (where manual release to accounting or collective settlement is required).
If you're not using a two-step contract type and accounting posting is blocked, the issue could stem from one of the following:

  • Calculation error during settlement execution – Check the settlement execution log and use WZR2 (Change Mode) to perform a "Check Document".
  • Account determination error – Review the account assignment in WZR3 (Display Mode).
  • Configuration inconsistency – For example, the process type might have been configured to allow warning (instead of error) when tax code is missing, which can cause posting blocks under certain conditions.

3. Final Settlement cannot be executed for consistency reason if the previous partial settlement was not release to accounting. Partial and Final Settlement can't be skipped for consistency reason. 

Regards, 
Gauthier Victor
